home.desc: Hundreds of thousands of people from every corner of the world — near and far, from all backgrounds — write to the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Every letter is sent to the Rebbe's resting place at the Ohel in Queens, NY, with faith and trust to receive blessing, guidance, and salvation.
home.maalaLink: The Value of Writing to the Rebbe
home.card1Body: For decades as Rebbe, he received letters from hundreds of thousands of people worldwide — and said: "Write to me about everything, including good news."
home.card2Body: Even after the Rebbe's passing, hundreds of thousands continue to write — letters, Pidyon Nefesh, and requests for blessing.
home.card3Body: Every letter is printed and sent to the Rebbe's resting place at the Ohel in Queens, New York — where it is placed on the Rebbe's Tzion, and many merit to see salvation and blessing.
home.step1Body: Washing hands, a moment of intention, giving charity, and making a good resolution — a "vessel" that holds the blessing.
home.step2Body: Open your heart and write in your own words — a request, gratitude, or prayer. "The more detail, the more praiseworthy."
home.step3Body: The letter is printed and sent to the Ohel — the place people turn to from all over the world.
home.ctaTitle: There is an address you can turn to
home.ctaDesc: The Rebbe saw every person as an entire world. Every letter that reaches the Tzion is a personal appeal — a request for blessing, guidance and salvation, with full trust that the Rebbe cares about every detail of every Jewish life.

write.uploadNote: Prefer to write by hand? At the bottom of the writing page you can upload a photo of your letter — and it will be sent to the Ohel.
write.chooseTitle: What would you like to write?
write.chooseDesc: Not sure which to choose? Choose based on when you're writing.
write.letterDesc: When there is no special occasion — you want to reach out to the Rebbe, share, request, or give thanks. In completely free language.
write.panDesc: Erev Rosh Hashana · Birthday · 19 Kislev · 12 Tammuz · 3 Tammuz · 10 Shvat
write.letterModeTitle: Write what is in your heart
write.placeholderPan:
Write your personal request here.

You may request health, livelihood, a match, nachas from children, spiritual matters — anything on your heart.
Write in third person, continuing from the opening formula.
write.placeholderLetter:
Write what is in your heart here.

There is no right format and no right words. You can write a request, gratitude, prayer, or anything personal you wish.
write.privacy: Completely private · Between you and the Rebbe
write.letterSentTo: The letter will be sent to the Rebbe's Tzion at the Ohel.
write.panSentTo: The Pan will be sent to the Rebbe's Tzion at the Ohel.
write.successLetter: Letter sent to the Holy Ohel
write.successPan: Pan sent to the Holy Ohel
write.errorPreview: Error generating preview — please try again
write.uploadSectionLabel: Wrote by hand? Upload a photo
write.uploadTitle: Click to upload a photo or file
write.uploadDesc: A handwritten photo, scan, or PDF
write.letterNusachOpening: To the honor of our Rebbe
write.letterStep1Body: Writing a letter to the Rebbe is a moment of closeness — like entering a 'Yechidus', a private audience. Let's take a few brief moments to prepare properly.
write.letterStep2Body: It is customary to wash hands before writing — a physical and spiritual preparation, signaling that we are about to do something holy.
write.letterStep3Body: It is customary to give charity before writing to the Rebbe — a coin, a donation, or any act of giving. Charity opens gates to receive blessing.
write.letterStep4Body: Write seriously and wholeheartedly. Imagine you are standing before the Rebbe face to face — which words would you choose? What would you truly request?
write.letterStep5Body: It is customary to make a good resolution — even a small one. It becomes the 'vessel' that holds the blessing you are requesting.
write.letterStep6Body: There is no right format and no right words. Write what is truly on your heart — a request, gratitude, a question, sharing. The Rebbe reads every word.
write.letterStep7Body: "The more detail, the more praiseworthy." Don't be brief. Tell the Rebbe the actual situation, what you feel, exactly what is requested. The fuller the letter — the more it "reaches."
write.panStep1Body: Writing a Pan is a genuinely spiritual act — not just a request, but a soul connection. A few brief preparations will improve your intention and create the right 'vessel'.
write.panStep2Body: Wash your hands before writing — an act of purity that signals we are about to do something above the ordinary.
write.panStep3Body: It is customary to give charity before writing a Pan — a coin, a donation, or any act of giving. Charity opens gates to receive blessing.
write.panStep4Body: The recommended time for writing is after the morning prayer — when a person is already in a state of intention and connection to holiness.
write.panStep5Body: Include — or decide in your heart — one good resolution. It creates a 'vessel' through which the blessing can take effect. It doesn't need to be grand — even small and sincere.
write.panStep6Body: The accepted Chabad formula: "Please arouse abundant mercy upon the soul of [name] son/daughter of [mother's name]" — written in third person, without writing "I". After the formula — continue in your own personal words.
write.panNusachFormula: Please arouse abundant mercy upon the soul of

ohel.desc: The place hundreds of thousands of people turn to every year — and the spiritual significance of writing to it.
ohel.def_p1: The Ohel is the resting place of the Lubavitcher Rebbe — Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson — who passed on 3 Tammuz 5754 (1994). Located in Queens, New York, hundreds of thousands of people from around the world visit it every year.
ohel.def_p2: In Chassidic teachings, the resting place of a righteous person is called an "Ohel" — and serves as a gate to pray, request, and connect to the Tzaddik even after his passing from the physical world.
ohel.def_hours: The Ohel is open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week — to everyone, at any hour.
ohel.def_address: Old Montefiore Cemetery, 121-83 Springfield Blvd, Queens, NY 11413
ohel.hist_title: The Rebbe himself — and the Ohel
ohel.hist_p1: Even during his lifetime, the Rebbe visited his father-in-law's Ohel — the Rayatz — with extraordinary frequency. He saw these visits as a central pillar of his holy work.
ohel.hist_p2: When asked why he didn't move to the Land of Israel, the Rebbe answered:
ohel.hist_quote: "My holy work is connected to the sacred Tzion of my father-in-law — I am there, and from there I act."
ohel.hist_source: From the Rebbe's words — in the Sikhot of his leadership years
ohel.hist_p3: After his own passing, his Ohel became that same place — the gate through which people continue to turn and receive.
ohel.yechida_title: Writing to the Rebbe — "Soul to soul"
ohel.yechida_p1: "Yechidus" in Chassidic language is a private, personal meeting between a Chassid and the Rebbe — a soul-to-soul encounter, without intermediaries. Visiting the Ohel, and even more so writing to the Rebbe, is considered a form of such Yechidus.
ohel.yechida_quoteDesc: "Here — he is present" · What the Rebbe said about his father-in-law's Ohel — applies to his own
ohel.yechida_p2: The soul is not bound to place and not tied to time. Whoever turns with true intention — the reaching-out arrives.
ohel.process_title: What happens to a letter that is sent
ohel.process_p1: Every letter written on this site is printed and physically sent to the Ohel — where it is placed next to the Rebbe's Tzion, as has always been the custom.
ohel.step1_desc: A letter or Pan — in free language or the traditional formula. The Rebbe asked for detail.
ohel.step2_desc: The letter reaches us, printed and ready to be placed.
ohel.step3_desc: The letter is placed at the holy Tzion — where letters from all over the world reside.
ohel.customs_title: Customs for visitors to the Ohel
ohel.customs_p1: Those who visit the Ohel in person — there are several accepted customs:
ohel.custom1_desc: It is customary to immerse in a Mikveh before visiting — spiritual preparation before approaching.
ohel.custom2_desc: A Gartel (belt) is worn upon entering the Ohel — a Chassidic custom of separating upper from lower.
ohel.custom3_desc: Leather shoes are removed — as a symbol of entering a holy place.
ohel.custom4_desc: A candle is lit — light symbolizing the Tzaddik's soul and the connection of souls.
ohel.custom5_desc: "Maaneh Lashon" is recited — traditional Psalms and prayers — and a Pan is placed.
ohel.cta_title: Write a letter to the Rebbe
ohel.cta_desc: Your letter will reach the holy Ohel — sent, placed, and received.
ohel.cta_info: What do you write in a letter?

mikhtav.desc: What the custom is, what to write, and why the words reach — even today.
mikhtav.def_title: What is a letter to the Rebbe
mikhtav.def_p1: For decades as Rebbe, the Lubavitcher Rebbe received letters from hundreds of thousands of people worldwide — Jews and non-Jews, Chassidim and those far from observance. They wrote about everything on their hearts: requests for blessing, spiritual questions, sharing joys, personal hardships.
mikhtav.def_p2: The Rebbe personally replied to every letter. The archive of "Igrot Kodesh" — the letters the Rebbe wrote — now spans dozens of volumes.
mikhtav.def_quote_source: The Lubavitcher Rebbe — from his many letters
mikhtav.diff_title: What is the difference between a letter and a Pan
mikhtav.diff_card1_desc: Completely free writing — in personal language, without a required formula. Speaking to the Rebbe like a close person: requesting, sharing, asking, thanking.
mikhtav.diff_card2_desc: Writing in the traditional, structured formula of Pidyon Nefesh — fixed opening, name + mother's name, then the request. A deeply rooted Chassidic practice.
mikhtav.today_p1: Even after the Rebbe's passing on 3 Tammuz 5754 (1994), hundreds of thousands of Jews continue to write to the Rebbe. The letters reach the Ohel — the Rebbe's Tzion in Queens, New York — where they are placed next to the Tzion.
mikhtav.today_p2: The Rebbe himself instructed: "The more detail, the more praiseworthy" — to write in detail, without abbreviation, as if standing before him in person.
mikhtav.quote_main: "It is understood and simple that I myself read every letter received in my name"
mikhtav.what_title: What to write in a letter to the Rebbe
mikhtav.what_p1: The Rebbe asked people to write about everything — even good news, not only in times of distress:
mikhtav.what_item1_desc: Health, livelihood, a match, children, success in studies, business matters — any request on the heart.
mikhtav.what_item2_desc: The Rebbe was happy to hear about joys, successes, a newborn child, a marriage. "Write to me about everything, including good news."
mikhtav.what_item3_desc: Life decisions, dilemmas, major decisions — the Rebbe answered them in the Igrot Kodesh.
mikhtav.what_item4_desc: You can simply share — tell about life, hardships, dreams. The Rebbe asked for detail.
mikhtav.how_item1_desc: "To the honor of our holy Rebbe" — this is how a letter to the Rebbe begins. In the closing: your full name and your mother's name.
mikhtav.how_item2_desc: There is no right format and no right words. Write in your language — Hebrew, Yiddish, English, any language. The Rebbe read letters in dozens of languages.
mikhtav.how_item3_desc: "The more detail, the more praiseworthy." Don't be brief — share the background, the situation, the exact request.
mikhtav.how_item4_desc: Before writing — washing hands, giving charity, and making one good resolution.
mikhtav.how_highlight_desc: The Rebbe always encouraged writing in detail and not cutting short — the more you write, the deeper your petition reaches
mikhtav.cta_title: Write a letter to the Rebbe now
mikhtav.cta_desc: "Write to me about everything, including good news" — the Lubavitcher Rebbe

pidyon.desc: Everything you need to know — the meaning, preparation, formula, and every halachic and traditional detail.
pidyon.def_p1: Pidyon Nefesh — abbreviated Pan — is a letter of blessing request that a Chassid presents to his Rebbe. The word "Pidyon" comes from the language of redemption and exchange — to exchange the bad for good through the intervention of the Tzaddik.
pidyon.def_p2: The Pan has a dual and deep meaning: on one hand, it is a request for blessing and help — both material and spiritual. On the other hand, it is a soul connection of the requester to the Rebbe — a deep spiritual bond created by the very act of writing and submitting.
pidyon.when_p1: A Pan can be written at any time — but there are times when the custom is especially heightened:
pidyon.when_item1_desc: The primary and central time for submitting Pans. At 'Kahal' — the second day of Rosh Hashana — thousands of Pans were submitted to the Rebbe by hand.
pidyon.when_item2_desc: 19 Kislev, 12 Tammuz, 3 Tammuz, 10 Shvat — occasions when the connection to the Rebbe intensifies and Pans are submitted.
pidyon.when_item3_desc: One's birthday is a personal time of divine favor — a fitting time for self-reflection, requesting, and renewing the connection with the Rebbe.
pidyon.when_quote: "Write to me about everything, including good news" — the Lubavitcher Rebbe
pidyon.prep_item1_desc: Wash your hands before writing the Pan — an act of purity and intention.
pidyon.prep_item2_desc: The recommended time is after the morning prayer, when a person is already in a state of intention and connection to holiness.
pidyon.prep_item3_desc: The Rebbe instructed to include in the Pan — or to decide in one's heart — a good resolution in some area. The "resolution" creates a "vessel" through which the blessing can take effect.
pidyon.prep_highlight_title: Without a resolution — the 'vessel' is missing
pidyon.prep_highlight_desc: In Chassidic teaching, a blessing needs a 'vessel' — a place to rest in. Making a good resolution (even a small one) creates that vessel.
pidyon.howto_item1_desc: Write on a clean white page, preferably one side only.
pidyon.howto_item2_desc: Write in ink (pen), not pencil. The writing should be as clear and legible as possible.
pidyon.howto_item3_desc: Begin with the accepted Pan formula (see below), and then write the request in personal language.
pidyon.howto_item4_desc: You may include any type of request — health, livelihood, a match, children, success in studies, spiritual matters.
pidyon.howto_item5_desc: End with your signature — Hebrew first name + mother's name.
pidyon.nusach_title: How to write a Pan — the accepted formula
pidyon.nusach_display_continue: Then continue to write the personal request in your own words.
pidyon.nusach_p1: The accepted Chabad formula for a Pan:
pidyon.nusach_formula: Please arouse abundant mercy upon the soul, spirit, and neshamah of [name] son/daughter of [mother's name]
pidyon.nusach_p2: After the formula — continue in personal and free language to write the request.
pidyon.nusach_rule1_desc: The Pan is written in third person — "so-and-so son/daughter of so-and-so" — and not "I".
pidyon.nusach_rule2_desc: It is important to include the full Hebrew name and the mother's name — "so-and-so son/daughter of so-and-so".
pidyon.nusach_rule3_desc: As with a regular letter — the Rebbe asked for detail. Write the exact request and the circumstances.
pidyon.nusach_image_caption_label: For example — a Pan written by the Rebbe himself to the Previous Rebbe
pidyon.nusach_image_alt: A Pan written by the Rebbe to the Previous Rebbe
pidyon.nusach_image_caption: The Pan written by the Lubavitcher Rebbe to the Previous Rebbe — the Rebbe Rayatz
pidyon.nusach_why_desc: Chassidim customarily do not write 'I' in a Pan — as writing 'I' symbolizes the ego. Instead of 'I request', one writes 'Please arouse mercy upon [name]' — in third person.
pidyon.name_p1: The name written in the Pan is always the Hebrew name only — followed by: the mother's name (not the father's).
pidyon.diff_title: What is the difference between a letter and a Pan
pidyon.diff_p1: A letter to the Rebbe is free writing in any language at any time — a request, gratitude, question, or sharing.
pidyon.diff_p2: A Pidyon Nefesh (Pan) is writing in a structured traditional formula, customary at special times like Erev Rosh Hashana, birthdays, and Chassidic dates.
pidyon.cta_title: Write a Pidyon Nefesh to the Rebbe
pidyon.cta_desc: "The more detail, the more praiseworthy" — the Lubavitcher Rebbe

maala.eyebrow: The value of writing to the Rebbe
maala.desc: For decades the Rebbe received letters from hundreds of thousands of people — and read every single one himself. Everything known about what a letter to the Rebbe does, and what the stories have told.
maala.writeCta: Write a Letter to the Rebbe
maala.ctaBadge: There is an address you can turn to
maala.ctaDesc: Whatever is on your heart — a request, prayer, gratitude, question. Without shame, without abbreviation. "The more detail, the more praiseworthy."
maala.ctaFooter: The letter is printed and sent to the Holy Ohel in Queens, New York
maala.s1_title: The Rebbe reads every letter — himself
maala.s1_p1: Since he took upon himself the burden of leadership, hundreds of thousands of letters were written to the Rebbe. The Rebbe testified about this himself:
maala.s1_p2: Not a secretary, not an assistant — the Rebbe himself. Every letter that arrived was read by him. Every reply that went out was written by him. "As has been the custom from generations past" — so did the Rebbes before him, and so the Rebbe continued.
maala.s1_p3: The Rebbe even asked and demanded that people write to him — something very evident from the common phrase in his letters: "After a long pause — your letter was received." He repeated this request in many gatherings and private audiences.
maala.s2_title: The letter doesn't burden — it brings joy
maala.s2_p1: During the visit of emissaries to the Holy Land in 5716, the emissaries told a certain personality that reading letters does not burden the Rebbe. When that person wrote to the Rebbe about it, the Rebbe confirmed it and added:
maala.s2_p2: The letter is not a burden — it is joy. The Rebbe didn't merely "endure" the letters; he rejoiced in them. Every piece of news, every update, every question — all were welcome.
maala.s3_title: "Pleased to know" — the bond that strengthens
maala.s3_p1: On 28 Menachem-Av 5710, the Rebbe wrote a letter that reflects deeply how alive the Rebbe was to the connection through letters — also from his own side:
maala.s3_p2: Even when the news is not good — the very act of maintaining the letter connection strengthens and diminishes the feeling of loneliness. Writing creates a living bond; not a one-time petition, but an ongoing relationship.
maala.s3_p3: Moreover: the Rebbe added there — "And it is known as explained in several books, that even if it is impossible to help materially, nevertheless a good thought and good wishes from one Jew to another is something."
maala.s4_title: The Rebbe recognized — from the letters
maala.s4_p1: There were people who for years wrote to the Rebbe in detail and at length, though they had never met him face to face. And remarkably, when they later came to the Rebbe — he mentioned this in his words:
maala.s4_c1_title: Rabbi Isser Klonsky — "You used to write many letters here"
maala.s4_c1_p1: Rabbi Isser Klonsky, rabbi of Givat Mordechai in Jerusalem, came to the Rebbe for the first time on Chanukah 5751. After years of an active letter correspondence between them — but they had never met face to face.
maala.s4_c1_p2: When he arrived, the Rebbe said to him:
maala.s4_c1_p3: The Rebbe recognized him — not from a photo, not from a prior direct acquaintance. From the letters. The writing had created a real presence. The Rebbe knew.
maala.s4_c2_title: Rabbi Moshe Shuster — "It has been a long time that we have had a letter correspondence"
maala.s4_c2_p1: One of the distinguished Belz Chassidim, Rabbi Moshe Shuster of Bnei Brak, who dedicated his life to acts of kindness, regularly sent the Rebbe large envelopes filled with the names of Jews in need of healing and salvation.
maala.s4_c2_p2: Year after year he sent, and never came to the Rebbe face to face. In his later years, when he came to the Rebbe for the first time, the Rebbe said to him:
maala.s4_c2_p3: For the Rebbe, this was not a first meeting. Between him and Rabbi Moshe — there was already a bond. A bond built letter by letter, envelope by envelope, year by year.
maala.s5_title: The Rebbe reads also what is between the lines
maala.s5_p1: The Rebbe didn't only read what was written — he also read what was hidden behind the words. On 29 Tammuz 5713 the Rebbe wrote: "I was pleased by what is evident between the lines of his letter..." And in 5736 he said in a private audience: "I received all your letters, I read them, and I also read what you wrote between the lines..."
maala.s5_p2: And in the special letter to Rabbi Efraim Eliezer Yales (Igrot Kodesh vol. 6, p. 1), the Rebbe wrote:
maala.s5_c1_title: The word that could not be read
maala.s5_c1_p1: It is told that once the Rebbe received a letter, and one word in it was not clear enough to read. The Rebbe circled that word and wrote beside it:
maala.s5_c1_p2: The Rebbe didn't skip it, didn't fill in the blank himself — he gently asked that people write clearly. Every word mattered to him.
maala.s5_c2_title: The Rebbe asked about the mikveh — answer immediately
maala.s5_c2_p1: It is told in the memoirs of one of the anash: a young Temimim student wrote to the Rebbe. The Rebbe read the letter — and immediately turned to his secretary, Rabbi Chadkov, and asked him to find out when was the last time that young man had immersed in the mikveh before writing to the Rebbe.
maala.s5_c2_p2: The Rebbe added: he wants to know the answer immediately.
maala.s5_c2_p3: Upon inquiry it emerged that indeed a long time had passed since that young man had gone to the mikveh. The Rebbe didn't only read what was written — he saw what was hidden behind the words, and asked precisely the right question.
maala.s6_p1: There were those who didn't write — and to the Rebbe this was heavy. He asked, wondered, sought explanation:
maala.s6_p2: "And if there is nothing to report — that itself is a great wonder." Meaning: there is always something to write about. If not, that itself is a question.
maala.s7_title: Not only in distress — write also with good news
maala.s7_p1: The Rebbe complained several times in his talks and letters about the type of Chassidim known as "Tzarot Chassidim" — those who come in writing only in times of distress:
maala.s7_p2: In a letter from 11 Shvat 5715 the Rebbe elaborated: "It would be good for all sides, if anash would get used to writing and reporting on every matter of joy, and this habit would become second nature, so that even by natural means a change would be felt in this direction, that joy would increase in their affairs."
maala.s7_p3: Writing about joys brings joy. The Rebbe wanted to hear also about marriages, children, successes, the spreading of the wellsprings — not only about troubles and requests.
maala.s8_p1: The Rebbe repeated this rule again and again in his letters and talks: to detail, to write, not to shorten.
maala.s8_p2: Name, mother's name, age, occupation, the situation, the request — everything. The Rebbe didn't want summaries. He wanted the whole person — with all the details, all the circumstances, everything on the heart.
maala.s8_p3: In a private audience (Nissan 5734) the Rebbe explained: "It is better to write with all the details, and then we will see what to answer" — because when a person writes himself, he writes all the details, and there is no concern that a detail will be missed that wasn't revealed to the one asking on his behalf.
maala.s9_p1: Beyond the effect that the letter has upon the Rebbe — there is an additional virtue in writing:
maala.s9_p2: If one knows that one must write to the Rebbe — one asks oneself: what will I write? And in that very question there is already self-reckoning. Writing creates commitment; commitment shapes behavior.
maala.s10_title: The Pan works — even before it arrives
maala.s10_p1: On Shabbat Parshat Pinchas 5711, the Rebbe said something that had been known among Chassidim for generations, and now the Rebbe confirmed and explained it:
maala.s10_p2: The very act of writing — the very decision to reach out — already opens a gate. One doesn't need to wait for the letter to arrive. The writing itself is the beginning of the effect.
maala.s11_title: "In an instant he knows everything" — the computer analogy
maala.s11_p1: At the end of Cheshvan 5747, the Rebbe wrote a rare and extraordinary response — a reply to the report from the emissaries' conference in South America, published in Likkutei Sichot vol. 25. In it the Rebbe explained in a remarkable way the effect of submitting at the Ohel:
maala.s11_p2: The Rebbe took the newest invention of his time — the computer — to explain the power of submitting at the Ohel. If a machine can "in an instant" know everything — how much more so when submitting to a Tzaddik, who is a soul in a body.
maala.s12_p1: On 5 Tevet 5747 the joy of "Didan Natzach" was celebrated — victory in the court case over the books. At the Maariv prayer of 6 Tevet, in the midst of the celebrations, the Rebbe suddenly turned toward the large crowd that filled the hall wall to wall, and began a brief talk:
maala.s12_p2: After having already left the study hall, the Rebbe returned to add: "All of the above applies to men, women, and children — our young and our old, our sons and our daughters."
maala.s12_c_title: The next morning — the crowd came to write
maala.s12_c_p1: Already from the morning the place filled with a large and varied crowd — Chassidim from various circles, accomplished individuals, and additional Jews, all writing Pans with reverence and awe, and submitting them at the secretariat whose staff worked feverishly without pause.
maala.s12_c_p2: Each person made sure to obtain the full names of their family members, relatives, and acquaintances. The names of over one hundred thousand (!) children registered in the worldwide "Tzivos Hashem" center were included for a blessing.
maala.s12_c_p3: Afterward the Rebbe left with a radiant face, and traveled to the Ohel — from which he returned only five hours later.
maala.s13_title: The Rebbe and Shazar — "Now I am no longer the boss"
maala.s13_p1: In the summer of 5726, Mr. Shazar visited the Rebbe, for the first time as President of the State of Israel. This visit was preceded by a web of political pressures applied to Shazar — not to come to the Rebbe, since it was not fitting for the "President of the State" to go to the Rebbe when the Rebbe did not leave 770.
maala.s13_p2: Upon his arrival in the United States, Shazar called the Rebbe and told him about the pressures. He asked the Rebbe to come to him, as his great desire was to see him face to face. The Rebbe replied:
maala.s13_p3: The next day, Friday, at three in the afternoon, the secretary Rabbi Chodakov called and connected Shazar with the Rebbe. The Rebbe and Shazar spoke in Russian, and the Rebbe said to him:
maala.s13_p4: Meaning — after visiting the Ohel, he was no longer the "boss" over the decision. Shazar was the one who needed to come to the Rebbe, not the other way around.
maala.s13_p5: When Shazar visited the Rebbe again, on 5 Shvat 5733, the Rebbe openly told him what he had heard at the Ohel. The Rebbe said to him in Yiddish: "Ikh bin geven Erev Rosh Chodesh oyfn 'Ohel' un der shver hot iberegebn az di nesi'ah vet zayn kashure" — "I was at the Ohel on Erev Rosh Chodesh, and my father-in-law [the Rebbe Rayatz] conveyed that the journey will go well." Shazar was deeply moved at that moment, and warmly pressed the Rebbe's hand.
maala.s14_c_title: Rabbi Dov-Yehuda Shochat — his daughter who fell into a pot of boiling water
maala.s14_c_p1: Around the time of 10 Shvat 5713, Rabbi Dov-Yehuda Shochat came to his first 'Yechidus' with the Rebbe — following a 'miracle' that happened to his young daughter.
maala.s14_c_p2: The daughter fell into a pot of boiling water. Most doctors gave her a short time to live. He wrote a Pan to the Rebbe. When he became frightened and wanted to shout at the doctors who were at fault for the deterioration — the Rebbe responded: "Narrishkeit" (foolishness).
maala.s14_c_p3: A short time later the girl recovered, to everyone's astonishment.
maala.s14_c_p4: When Rabbi Shochat came to the Rebbe to give thanks, the Rebbe directed him to the Ohel and said:
maala.s14_c_p5: The Rebbe directed the thanks to the holy Ohel — for it was there that the Pan arrived, and there that the salvation was given.
maala.s15_title: Today too — hundreds of thousands of Jews write
maala.s15_p1: Even today, after the passing, hundreds of thousands of Jews write Pans, letters, reports, and blessing requests — including good news — to the Rebbe. The letters are sent to the holy Ohel from every corner of the world, from Chassidim, emissaries, and "your people the House of Israel" — men, women, and children.
maala.s15_p2: The Rebbe, as a "faithful shepherd," has not, God forbid, abandoned his flock. As then so today, and even "more than during his lifetime" — the Rebbe cares for every single member of the Jewish people in general and for Chassidim in particular: reads the letters, rejoices at times of joy, feels pain when there is pain, arouses mercy and works for our salvation.
maala.s15_h3: feels your pain — as then, so today.
maala.q1_text: It is understood and simple that I myself read all the letters received in my name, and I also answer them myself . . as has been the custom from time immemorial and from generation to generation
maala.q2_text: And the emissaries were certainly right that these letters do not weigh upon me, and may we merit, among all Israel, that the content of letters between one person and another should be joyful both materially and spiritually and in good that is seen and revealed
maala.q3_text: I am pleased to know what is happening with the Anash, may they live, for if the news is good then it brings comfort and joy, and even if, G-d forbid, the opposite — in any case this itself — maintaining the epistolary connection — strengthens, and makes the feeling of loneliness smaller
maala.q4_c1_quote: You used to write here many letters...
maala.q4_c2_quote: For a long time we have had a connection through letters. Blessed is G-d that we merit to meet face to face...
maala.q5_text: And as he requested, I read his note at the holy resting place . . and surely the Rebbe [Previous] will arouse mercy regarding the matters he wrote in the note, and also regarding what is 'between the lines' and also what is hinted in the whiteness of the paper
maala.q5_source: The Rebbe — Igrot Kodesh vol. 6, p. 1
maala.q5_c1_quote: Pardon — just as 'clear speech' is a virtue, clear handwriting is the same
maala.q6_text: Why does he not report anything of what happens to him and in his surroundings for good? And what is the purpose of the melancholy and its companions (if that is the cause of his silence)? And if there is nothing to report — that itself is a very great wonder
maala.q6_source: From a letter of the Rebbe to one of the Anash
maala.q7_text: There are those who correspond only, unfortunately, when some sorrow and grief, G-d forbid, has occurred
maala.q8_source: The Rebbe — 26 Kislev 5719 (and in many other letters)
maala.q9_text: When a chassid knows that he is obligated to write to the Rebbe from time to time — this causes him to conduct himself as he should in all his matters
maala.q9_source: From the Rebbe's words; and also: 'We see that the knowledge that over time one's activities will be written about — brings an increase in activities, whether in quantity or quality' (9 Iyar 5719)
maala.q10_text: As is known among chassidim, when one writes a Pidyon and sends it to the Rebbe, the effect is already accomplished even if the Pidyon has not yet reached the Rebbe's hands and he has not seen it with his physical eyes — the chassid must do his part, commit himself to the Rebbe and trust in him, and when he does his part and from his side there is no impediment, then the matter is already accomplished by the Rebbe
maala.q11_text: That immediately upon delivering the letter to the Rebbe — all matters connected to it are completed... To make it easier to understand — a vivid example precisely in our times: when one enters data into a computer... in an instant all conclusions and rulings are actually known. And when one delivers — mentions at the resting place (of the Previous Rebbe) — this is incomparably greater than the delivery to the inanimate object mentioned, incomparably greater
maala.q11_source: The Rebbe — end of Cheshvan 5747, Likutei Sichot vol. 25
maala.q12_text: And to add, that since there will be no possibility to read all the notes, but only to place them and deliver them at the resting place — each and every person can write whatever he wants (without any shame), for this is in the manner of writing to the Almighty, through the leader of our generation!...
maala.q13_quote1: Tomorrow I will be at the Ohel and only afterwards will I be able to respond
maala.q13_quote2: Now I am no longer the master

contact.desc: The site is still in the process of being built and developed — and we are happy to hear from you.
contact.status_title: In the process of building and development
contact.status_p1: We are working on building a platform that will allow every Jew — anywhere in the world — to send a letter to the Rebbe easily, respectfully, and in the right way.
contact.status_p2: The site is active and receiving letters, but still developing — and there is always more to improve, add, and beautify.
contact.help_item1_desc: The project operates with server costs, letter sending, and development. Any support helps us continue and grow.
contact.help_item2_desc: Developers, designers, marketing people — we'd love to hear from you.
contact.help_item3_desc: Do you have an idea for improvement, a feature worth adding, or something that didn't work well? We'd love to know.
contact.cta_desc: For any question, suggestion, collaboration request, or anything else — write to us directly.
